text: The surgeon begins the robotic-assisted Ivor Lewis esophagectomy by placing ports in the abdomen. A camera port is inserted approximately two centimeters above the umbilicus, and a Nathanson retractor is used to retract the liver. A twelve millimeter port is positioned in the right upper quadrant, while two eight millimeter ports are placed in the left upper quadrant. A five millimeter assist port may be added for suctioning.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to carefully divide the greater omentum from the transverse colon, identifying a vascular pedicle consisting of a vein and an artery off the gastroepiploic artery. A tip-up grasper retracts tissue from the right-hand port, while a bipolar dissector and a fenestrated bipolar are used for dissection and sealing.

The surgeon carefully dissects the omentum from the colon, ensuring not to harvest excessive tissue to avoid bulkiness. The omental flap is visible, flipped over the stomach in a cephalad direction. The dissection continues towards the pylorus, with attention to the pedicle of the right gastroepiploic artery, using bipolar cautery to navigate through the fatty tissue.

The surgeon continues along the greater curvature of the stomach, dividing the short gastric vessels using a vessel sealer. An assistant uses a 5mm grasper to retract the pancreas and colon. The view shows the stomach being retracted down towards the umbilicus while additional attachments to the omentum and spleen are taken down.

The surgeon approaches the left cruce without dividing it, focusing on the remaining short gastric attachments. The stomach is lifted anteriorly, exposing the left gastric vein and artery. The vein is dissected and divided using a vessel sealing device, while the artery is handled with caution, potentially using hemlock clips.

The surgeon uses the robotic system to dissect the left gastric artery, opening the lesser omentum to access the lesser sac. A large replaced left hepatic artery is visible, and the surgeon carefully skeletonizes it to identify branches leading to the stomach. A proximal branch is clipped with a hemlock clip, and the vessel sealer is used to divide the artery.

The surgeon identifies vagal branches and proceeds to take tissue cephalad to the common artery, removing it with the specimen. The right cruce is identified, and tissue is dissected down to the level of the descending aorta. Celiac axis lymph nodes are excised, and fat is divided to expose the common hepatic artery. Blunt dissection is used to create planes, avoiding the portal vein. The left gastric vein is visible descending towards the portal vein. Tissue is taken down to the inferior vena cava (IVC) and removed as a separate specimen. A tracheal suction catheter is used through a five millimeter port for controlled suction. Tissue is divided at the level of the crow's foot, and a conduit is created using robotic staplers.

The surgeon uses a green load stapler to create a narrow gastric conduit, approximately five centimeters wide, ensuring no twisting occurs during staple firing. The conduit is divided from the proximal stomach and sutured to the stomach remnant for chest placement. Indocyanine green dye is administered to assess conduit perfusion, visibly confirming adequate blood flow except for a small area at the top. A stitch is placed at the watershed area to guide anastomosis placement below it. The omental flap is also shown to be well-perfused.

The surgeon performs a hiatal dissection, carefully dissecting down onto the aorta while maintaining a radial margin. The pericardium is exposed, and the dissection extends towards the carina without dividing the cruci or hiatus. The existing jejunostomy tube is noted, eliminating the need for additional laparoscopic placement.

The surgeon mobilizes approximately six to seven centimeters of tissue up into the hiatus to facilitate chest dissection. A chest tube is inserted through an abdominal port into the left pleura using a Blake drain. The tip of the conduit and the omental flap are sewn to the stomach remnant for elevation into the chest. Botulinum toxin is injected into the pylorus.

The patient is rotated to the left lateral acute position for the chest portion of the esophagectomy. Ports are placed, including a camera port over the sixth rib, an eight millimeter port posteriorly, another inferiorly, and one above the fourth or third rib. The inferior pulmonary ligament is dissected down, staying close to the pericardium, and the tissue at the hiatus is resected en bloc with the esophageal specimen.

The surgeon uses a bipolar dissecting device to carefully divide the pleura along the anterior and posterior aspects of the esophagus, reaching up to the bronchus. The esophagus is divided just below the azygos vein, in the region of the gastroesophageal junction, due to the presence of a C-word type two tumor. The thoracic duct is also taken along with the specimen, with the aorta visible deep to the duct.

The surgeon approaches the thoracic duct, clips it using hemlock clips, and proceeds to remove the subcarinal lymph nodes en bloc with the esophageal specimen. The nodes are carefully lifted from the pericardium to minimize bleeding, although their vascularization can make extraction challenging.

The surgeon clips the thoracic duct at its cephalad aspect while retracting the esophagus laterally. The remaining attachments to the aorta and pericardium are carefully dissected using a vessel sealing device through the posterior port. The left main stem bronchus is visible, with the left-sided endotracheal tube in place. Subacromial nodes are elevated to expose the esophagus musculature for division. Endocyanin green and near-infrared imaging confirm a well-perfused portion of the esophagus for division, which is performed using endoshears.

The surgeon enlarges the anterior incision to remove the esophageal specimen and manually pulls the conduit up from the proximal stomach, ensuring the omentum is sutured to the stomach to facilitate this process. The conduit is carefully maneuvered to avoid excessive tension, reaching the level of the azygos vein. A purse-string anastomosis is performed using two 3-0 Prolene sutures, with the first being a running horizontal mattress suture that incorporates both muscle and mucosa.

The surgeon positions a 25mm circular stapler without the anvil in the esophagus lumen, using a needle driver to carefully tie the proline suture for the second purse string. A small gastronomy is made at the tip of the conduit, and the stapler is inserted through the conduit, exiting along the greater curvature. Suction is used to manage any spillage of gastric contents.

The camera is repositioned to the cephalad-most port for a better view of the stapler exiting the back of the gastric conduit. The surgeon uses a ProGrasp instrument to manipulate the anvil, ensuring no tissue is incorporated into the anastomosis. The assistant fires the stapler, and the surgeon checks the completeness of the doughnuts, taking care to avoid ischemic areas near the staple line.

The surgeon over-sews the staple line at the more ischemic end, imbricating it for reinforcement. Moving to the hiatus, two or three tacking sutures are placed to secure the stomach to the diaphragm edge, using silk or Vicryl sutures. The omental flap is positioned to wrap around the conduit, securing it to pleura or mediastinal tissue to prevent migration. A chest tube is inserted, the cavity is irrigated, and the lung is inflated.
